sentences.text: To be quite honestly, I was not upset about that.
I would have liked to go with them, but said-- as said previously, I found it dangerous.
And I was thinking about getting out from France, joining the-- to get to England at the time, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which, of course, didn't work out.
I was alone, but very-- very shortly afterwards, my cousin managed to join me, cousin Kowalski, Grisha Kowalski.
And we were together then, for a while.
In Toulouse, I stayed up in a flat what my parents took when they came.
But I wasn't so long in Toulouse, because very soon, the French authorities forced us to leave.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because originally, we were in that flat.
But then they started moving the refugees.
And they sent us what they call [FRENCH], what means-- it's a French word what means that you are allowed to go to some spot and not move further.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And you're under supervision of the gendarme, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] what they have in-- in most places in France.
They were in charge of security.
And you must report to them once a week.
Only in Toulouse.
Because I was sent away, and then he went away to the south of France, where eventually, he found refuge in a farm near Aix-en-Provence, what is not very far from Marseilles.
Toulouse to Luchon.
No, I went by train.
And I had to report to the gendarme.
No, I wasn't escorted.
But they gave me what they call a [FRENCH], that you're allowed to go.
In case they check in the train, or whatever it is, they shouldn't worry me.
Toulouse to Luchon-- I would say it was a couple of hours by train.
That's on the Spanish border by the Pyrenees.
There were people there, quite a-- a lot of refugees-- not from Belgium.
From Belgium, there might have been just one, a few people, but mostly French people from Paris, from other parts.
And quite a number of Jews were there.
No, it wasn't.
This was a really a-- a winter resort, that place, what they used for the facilities there.
Well, I managed to get some accommodation.
And I was there quite a while, till eventually, I got to notice I must leave that place.
I must have been there about a year.
Less than a year-- there wasn't much to be done.
I used-- I had a little bit money on me.
And I used to get a little grant from the Red Cross from-- through America.
It came from England, really.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That time, life wasn't so expensive.
If you're not extravagant, you could live-- live-- you could live on it.
This was 19-- towards the end of 1940.
Till-- about a year, yes.
No, I tried to get away.
And I tried to cross the Spanish border, the Pyrenees, but we were caught.
At the border, when we came to Spain, we thought they'd let us through.
And we managed to bribe the-- the Spanish guards there.
And they let us return without turning us over to-- to the Germans.
I was-- I went with another man there, in fact.
Yeah.
Well, a man from Paris.
A Jewish man also.
Yes, by foot.
Well, the Pyrenees was-- you know, because the Pyrenees, it's feasible there.
There's some parts what are not so complicated.
We were not going in the high peaks.
Yeah, it was at night.
It took a couple of hours till we came to the other side of Spain.
Well, we were-- then I went back to Luchon.
And then, eventually, we were notified that we had to leave-- that I had to leave at least, not we.
Had to go in Lacaune.
It was quite a long way, and--
That was also train.
It was a town, a little French town.
Well, I managed to get some accommodation and stay there for a while.
And that's where I got in touch with some other French people there.
Well, in there, we remained a day till about the end of the war.
Well, that's when I had my-- changed my papers.
I got French identity papers.
Well, in-- in France-- at least in the country-- it's-- it's quite an easy process.
You go to what they call, in French, a [FRENCH].
Because in French, there is a-- [FRENCH] is a government business.
Only they are allowed to sell.
And they give you the forms, the ID papers.
You must get your ID papers.
And these papers you must take to the [FRENCH], what is the mayor's place.
You fill it out.
And it must be stamped and identified by them with photo and fingerprints.
This, of course, is feasible in little places like this.
You couldn't do it in Paris where you would have to go to the prefecture.
They wouldn't trust you to do it.
But there, you could bring your own papers.
And they identify it, and they make it legal.
Yes, I did.
Well, I looked in a-- in a phone directory.
I want to take an area what shouldn't be suspicious.
And I took an area in the north of France what occupied by the German.
Because whatever it is, the Vichy were not checking what is there, the Vichy authorities.
And I took a name and an address from there.
And that's what I used for my papers.
I took the name of Thibault.
Joseph Thibault I took.
I wanted to have the same initials.
Yes. 'Thibault' is T-H-I-B-A-U-L-T.
No, they didn't know.
No, they didn't take it for-- they were not suspicious about it.
There might be other people, but they wouldn't say so.
I wouldn't know.
I was on my own there, yes.
What happened?
After that, well, I got some contact.
And I think they helped me around, you know?
The people were connected with the French Resistance.
Well, it was a lady what I met to be honest.
She was, what they call in France, a letter box.
A letter box is a-- a mailbox for the Resistance, you know?
Messages and all this, you know?
Just-- just by-- I met her at a-- I stayed at a little hotel, and she was staying, also, there.
And we became friendly.
She offered me-- she had a lot of contacts.
And this helped me a lot.
Not altogether.
I helped in a few little incidents.
Oh, incidents-- moving the items and various things what I had to do.
It was-- I wouldn't say it was armed incidents, but moving the documentation and other things.
In the area.
Had to go to-- I went to Toulouse, back, you know, and other places-- not Toulouse.
Where is-- [FRENCH].
Indirectly.
Because there were some German raids also in the area, you know?
And the marquis came down.
And there were various things were to be fixed.
By having connection, they gave you information, what to do and where to go.
And I wouldn't see the German Wehr in that area particularly in force.
They were not.
But they used to-- they used to come down occasionally, checking.
Because it was a little locality what they were not very much interested in.
Yes, I was.
Well, but there's very little to say.
Because when, at some stage, the whole town was, like, encircled, I managed to find some old Frenchman who-- and I stayed with him there.
And he-- then he hid me there.
In a house, yeah.
No, it wasn't long, because these raids were only-- they were coming and going.
They were not searching entirely.
They were going to big towns that would have had maintenance and everything.
But in those little places, I suppose they didn't have the-- the manpower to do it.
They just came through.
They sent a squad or some few vehicles they send, and then they left.
Well, this was a particular house where the French guy was.
Yeah, it was a house.
In fact, it was a double-level house, you know, in the hill there.
Not especially.
I was in-- I was there in that area, but not especially.
Well, there are alarms more than once, but it worked out right.
Till the end of the war.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I would say from about-- from '41 till '45-- end of '41.
Well, I didn't have to be in hiding always.
It's only when the Germans came around.
I stayed-- I had a room there by a French guy there.
There wasn't much to be done.
I used to help occasionally in there, do a little manual work, but not much.
No, not for Jewish.
They might be inquisitive why I was there the whole time.
But they didn't show it officially.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Because there were a few other refugees-- not Jews.
In fact, one family was Jewish.
It was a fellow who made pictures in Paris.
He was a-- a filmmaker.
He was hiding there also, yes, with his family.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]
Well, one was a man called Minot-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] M-I-N-O-T.
He was my main factor there.
No.
Well, I don't suppose he would be alive at that time.
He wasn't young at the time.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it would be useless to try to make contact, because I'm sure he couldn't be alive.
I did.
I got-- I was in contact, through one other way, with Morocco.
They must have found my address.
Because-- because when we left Belgium, we made some arrangements where we could eventually hide.
One, in fact, was-- I kept, and I can't remember.
It was a wholesale firm what used to be a customer of my uncle, a Jewish wholesaler, and also, a cousin we had in Boston.
I would imagine, rather, through Boston, we got-- they got my address.
They wrote occasionally, yeah, from Morocco.
Well, that's the only contact I used to get still, from Belgium.
I couldn't get news.
And-- and then they were deported.
And that was the end of it.
In Morocco there was-- there were two of my aunties with their family, and also my granny.
They came to Morocco.
But when they left Belgium, they landed, eventually, in Bayonne, what is towards Spain and Atlantic Sea.
And they managed to get on a boat what was still leaving at that time.
Where that boat was going, nobody knew.
Eventually, the boat landed in Morocco, French Morocco.
Well, now it's independent, but that time, it was French still.
That's when the war started with the East and all this.
And part of the fleet managed to go.
Part of the fleet were-- was destroyed, the French fleet.
Another part didn't listen to the orders.
And they went to-- to Africa, you know?
And that's where they landed eventually-- landed, eventually, in Marrakesh, and then to Mogador, where they also stayed till the end of the war.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were also afraid at the time, because the German were battling to get hold of Africa at that point and eventually defeat it.
When Rommel was defeated, then they felt more secure.
Joseph, did you ever hear from your family in Antwerp?
In wartime?
No more.
I got some message at the beginning, and that was the end of it.
Well, like, one or the letter came through, you know?
This was when I was still in Toulouse.
And then-- well, I suppose, first, letters were intercepted, you know?
And you couldn't get through.
And that was the end.
I never heard, afterwards, from them.
Well, at that time-- well, they-- they wrote carefully already, at that time, things [? you ?] [? couldn't ?] [? right ?.] At the beginning, it seemed-- things seemed to be all right.
This was at the first stage.
They got back.
And I don't know what they managed to do exactly, because it didn't last long, unfortunately.
Then they had to get-- they went into hiding.
As I-- as I mentioned to you, we had a very faithful castle what we trusted 100%.
Because we helped them, from nothing, to build up a tremendous business.
And I caught, in my information, these people betrayed them.
In fact, when I went back to Belgium after the war, when I came to know what was right and wrong, I wanted to make a court case to claim some damage just to have moral satisfaction.
But they-- they declared themself insolvent.
And the Belgium lawyers-- if you claim any amount, whatever it is, so many percent you must pay to the government even if you get money.
But once they declare themself insolvent, there was no purpose to make any claim.
I made a moral claim, what I called one franc, what means nothing.
It's just a moral claim.
And I couldn't do anything that means-- I couldn't get any satisfaction.
Those people are still there.
They've got their business-- never mind where they declared insolvent.
And whatever they could steal, they stole.
There were surely other things.
I know their name.
I went to see them.
They put a big act up.
And-- and that's how far it went.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And maybe if they would have played the game, my parents would have been alive today.
No, not in Luchon.
Luchon was about a year.
Yes.
Yeah.
There it was a longer time.
Well, there were quite incident.
There was fighting incidents with fights with the German forces-- not exactly in the locality where I was, but in the area.
We were quite-- periodically, I would say, there were incidents.
It was a hilly area, forests, mountains there.
And there were quite a number of hiding places.
And the German didn't like to go too far there, because they didn't, always, secure.
Then, incidents happened quite often.
Part only.
Well, there were some fatalities, of course.
And then the German were leaving.
Always, they-- they were not remaining.
It was the big area.
It wasn't-- it wasn't a big area, and it was no industry there.
It was no business area mainly.
Then, it returned to tranquility after they left-- typical country area.
I wouldn't say that the population was pro-German.
I wouldn't say that.
They were very anti German, but not in, like, some area where they had what they call a lot of collaborators.
This was, I would say, a-- I would say that more than 99% was anti-German.
And we were always hoping, of course, Germany will get defeated.
Yes.
No, it was a very minor part.
Well, just assisting.
It was paperwork, moving items like-- mainly paperwork.
No, no, not office.
No, there were people coming around, you know?
You had to hand over documentation and things like this.
Well-- well, I didn't read it.
But it was documentation about movement, of German movement, things happening in the area.
Because they had-- in the mountains, there was quite a strong group of the Resistance, the Maquis.
Pardon?
M-A-Q-U-I-S.
Well, there was sabotage, you know?
They were mining railways, bridges.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
Yes.
Well, we were not allowed to listen to the radio.
But it was what we called the de Gaulle movement, you know?
They had the regular broadcast to France.
That's when they used to play that famous tune from Beethoven, The Fifth Symphony.
This was the opening tune.
At the beginning, we didn't know.
But afterwards, when we heard the radio, we knew.
We knew-- at the first stage, we didn't know what's happening.
Nobody was aware.
But coming from England, the news were there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This was about '42.
Well, it started early '42, you know, that we knew exactly-- not exactly.
We knew quite a bit of news, information about the-- the fate of the Jews.
Well, they were upset about this.
I wouldn't say everybody believed it.
Because in fact, at some stage, when I managed to get contact with my cousin, what I was-- I went to see him in that farm where he was.
It was an old man, the father.
Then the son told him, well, you know, that man is hiding here because the German want to-- [INAUDIBLE] was, well if-- well, if the German want to kill him, that he deserves it.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
Because in his mentality, a country mentality, they couldn't imagine that the Germans could be so bad, right?
They could understand this war, but not to kill people just for the sake because they're Jewish.
Joseph, were you still trying to escape from France at this time?
Yes, but I didn't know how to do it at the time.
I was trying to get contact, you know?
But I couldn't get it.
They were going to try to help me, but I couldn't get a right way to go.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
No, I made contact.
Well, first was a family what I knew there, what I knew-- not before, what I met there, and some French people what I met.
